patches.find: Unified angular coordinate system is a novel prediction.
patches.replace: Dome's unified angular coordinate system with scale factor 0.9941 reduces cross-equatorial distance errors within the dome's own V13 framework.
patches.rationale: ISS-059: Original claim was vague. New text reflects what dome actually claims — a coordinate system with a specific fitted parameter.

patches.find: A unified coordinate transformation is an internal model convention—a mathematical re-parametrization of the assumed geometry. Coordinate choices are arbitrary — see Torge & Müller (2012), Geodesy; transforming from one system to another is not a physical prediction. Coordinates are labels for points in space, not physical phenomena. The change from one coordinate system to another has no observable consequences unless it alters the model's physical predictions.
patches.replace: The unified angular coordinate with scale factor 0.9941 was not derived a priori from dome geometry — it was fitted post-hoc to match WGS84 geodetic distances on six known routes. Any free parameter fitted to known data will reduce residuals; the question is whether 0.9941 was predicted before measuring. The dome site provides no derivation from disc radius, firmament height, or aetheric properties — only the fitted result. Furthermore, the V13 coordinate system still requires WGS84 distances as input (see the model's own OPEN-001 and OPEN-003 acknowledgments), making it dependent on globe geodesy rather than independent of it. Standard geodesy achieves sub-0.01% distance accuracy worldwide (Torge & Müller, 2012); the dome's 6.2% RMSE is 600× worse even after fitting.
patches.rationale: ISS-059: Addresses dome's actual claim (fitted parameter). ISS-060: No longer 'proves too much' — focuses on post-hoc fitting. ISS-061: Torge & Müller now correctly cited as benchmark for geodetic precision.

patches.find: Coordinate choice is convention, not a physical prediction testable against nature.
patches.replace: The 0.9941 scale factor is a post-hoc fit to WGS84 distances, not a prediction derived from dome geometry. Any model with a free parameter can reduce residuals on known data; this is curve-fitting, not prediction.
patches.rationale: ISS-059: Verdict text now addresses the actual claim rather than dismissing coordinates generically.

patches.find: Cepheid variable stars (see Pantheon+ / SH0ES) and surface brightness fluctuation (SBF) measurements are direct geometric distance indicators, independent of redshift. These are among the most reliable distance anchors in cosmology. Their agreement with the Hubble diagram (distance vs. redshift) validates both the distance scale and the expansion law. The dome model has no mechanism for generating cosmological redshift and no equation relating distance to it.
patches.replace: Cepheid variable stars and surface brightness fluctuation (SBF) measurements are standard candle distance indicators — they use calibrated luminosity relations, not direct geometry. Both form key rungs of the cosmic distance ladder, anchored to geometric parallax (see Riess et al. 2022, SH0ES; Blakeslee et al. 2021, SBF survey). Gaia DR3 parallaxes independently confirm Cepheid distances to ~1%, making the distance scale framework-independent. These measurements place galaxies at 10–100 Mpc (30–300 million light-years) — orders of magnitude beyond the dome's firmament at 9,086 km. The dome model has no spatial framework for where these galaxies exist, no mechanism for cosmological redshift, and no equation relating distance to it. See also WIN-047, which claims the same distance-redshift relationship using different standard candles — a salami-slicing duplication (Section 6.1, Pattern #4).
patches.rationale: ISS-125: Fixes 'geometric' → 'standard candle' error. Adds proper citations, Gaia cross-validation, scale problem (galaxies vs firmament), and WIN-047 cross-reference.

patches.find: Standard cosmological observations; dome has no redshift mechanism
patches.replace: Standard candle distances (Cepheids, SBF) anchored to Gaia parallax; dome has no distance framework beyond 9,086 km
patches.rationale: Differentiates from WIN-047 finding and emphasizes the scale problem.

patches.find: Two-zone disc topology improves northern hemisphere accuracy.
patches.replace: Two-zone disc topology reduces cross-equatorial distance errors from 25–78% (V12) to 6.2% RMSE (V13).
patches.rationale: ISS-136: Reflects dome's actual claim — cross-equatorial improvement, not NH improvement.

patches.find: V13 coordinates regressed NH accuracy from 5.2% to 7.3%
patches.replace: Cross-equatorial fit improved by adding free parameter (equatorial ring radius); still 600× worse than standard geodesy
patches.rationale: ISS-137: Addresses the actual claim (cross-equatorial) rather than the side-effect (NH regression).

patches.find: Version 13 of the model introduced two-zone topology to fit data more precisely. However, introduction of this feature actually degraded northern hemisphere accuracy from 5.2% error (V12) to 7.3% error (V13) — compare Vincenty (1975) geodetic formulae achieving sub-0.5% error. The change worsened performance in one hemisphere while claiming overall improvement, indicating a shift in fitting strategy rather than genuine predictive progress.
patches.replace: Version 13 introduced two-zone topology with a new free parameter (equatorial ring radius = 14,105 km) to fit cross-equatorial routes. The improvement from 25–78% to 6.2% RMSE is real but expected: adding a free parameter to fit six known routes will always reduce residuals. This is curve-fitting, not prediction. Meanwhile, NH accuracy degraded from 5.2% to 7.3% — the added complexity helped one region while hurting another. Compare Vincenty (1975) geodetic formulae achieving sub-0.01% error globally with zero fitted parameters — the dome's best result is still 600× worse. The dome's own OPEN-001 concedes the coordinate system still requires WGS84 as input.
patches.rationale: ISS-136/137: Addresses cross-equatorial claim directly, explains why improvement is expected (free parameter), retains NH regression as supporting evidence.

patches.find: The dome model provides no unified equations describing how two poles arise from its toroidal geometry, how they couple, or why they should have similar strengths despite the extreme area asymmetry between the pole aperture and the rim return path. Contrast this with CHAOS-7 and IGRF-13, which predict both poles — and the full multipolar structure — from a single spherical harmonic expansion of the geodynamo. The transition from V50.6's monopolar vortex to V51.0's toroidal architecture was not motivated by any failed prediction or new observation; the change is asserted rather than derived.
patches.replace: The dome model provides no unified equations describing how two poles arise from its toroidal geometry, how they couple, or why they should have similar strengths despite the extreme area asymmetry between the pole aperture and the rim return path. Contrast with CHAOS-7 and IGRF-13, which predict both poles and the full multipolar structure from a single spherical harmonic expansion of the geodynamo, achieving ~50 nT RMS globally (~0.1% of typical field strength). The dome's fitted B(r) claims to reduce RMS from 61% to 20% — but 20% RMS means ~10,000–13,000 nT error, which is 200× worse than IGRF-13. The transition from V50.6's monopolar vortex to V51.0's toroidal architecture represents a genuine mechanism change (monopole → toroid), but the change was not motivated by any failed prediction or new observation; it is asserted rather than derived from a detected flaw in V50.6.
patches.rationale: NEW-001: Fixes garbled sentence, quantifies IGRF comparison, acknowledges mechanism change is real while noting it's unmotivated.

patches.find: Intrinsic luminosity difference (F7Ib supergiant at 433 ly vs. F0III giant at 294 ly); Gaia photometry shows no systematic southern dimming
patches.replace: Intrinsic luminosity difference: Polaris is an F7Ib supergiant (~1,260 L☉, 433 ly) vs. Sigma Octantis F0III/IV (~40 L☉, 294 ly); Gaia photometry shows no systematic southern dimming
patches.rationale: NEW-002: Removes the erroneous 'Both stars ~130 ly' claim (which was in an older version) and adds specific luminosity values for clarity. The finding now correctly states the actual distances.
